mnemonics.title: RAM Types
mnemonics.content:
SRAM: Static - Super Fast, Really Awesome Memory
DRAM: Dynamic - Deep, Dense, Dollar-Saving Memory

mnemonics.title: ROM Types
mnemonics.content:
PROM: Programmably Written Once
EPROM: Erase Programmably, Repeatedly
EEPROM: Electrically Erase - Every Byte Precisely

mnemonics.title: Memory Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Registers Really Can Facilitate Rapid Access - R -> Cache -> RAM -> Secondary

acronyms.usage: Primary volatile memory for active programs and data
Non-volatile memory for firmware and boot software
Fast cache memory using flip-flop circuits
Main memory using capacitors requiring refresh
ROM that can be programmed once after manufacturing
ROM that can be erased using UV light and reprogrammed
ROM that can be electrically erased and reprogrammed in circuit
Cache memory levels with decreasing speed but increasing size

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted pyramid with Registers at top (smallest, fastest), then Cache, then RAM, then Secondary Storage at bottom (largest, slowest). Each level going down doubles in size but halves in speed.
SRAM: Draw a flip-flop circuit (6 transistors). DRAM: Draw a capacitor with one transistor. Show how SRAM maintains state while DRAM leaks and needs refresh.
Three parallel lines representing Address Bus (points to location), Data Bus (carries the actual data), and Control Bus (carries read/write signals). Show how they connect CPU to Memory.

analogies.analogy: Registers are like the desk in your workspace - you keep the documents you're working on right now for immediate access.
Cache is like a small tray on your desk where you keep frequently used papers - faster to grab than searching through filing cabinets (RAM).
RAM is like your workspace desk - large enough to hold active projects but cleared when you leave for the day.
ROM is like permanent instructions printed in a manual - it doesn't change and is always available regardless of power.
Volatile is like writing on a whiteboard (wipes clean when power off). Non-volatile is like writing in a notebook (stays even when power off).
